title: A Global Liquidity Crisis Is Underway… What’s Next?

When we come back, we're going to zero
in on markets. Futures are down this
morning kicking off the new week. Oil
prices is are up as time is running out
on the president's 48-hour deadline for
Tehran to reopen that Strait of Hormuz.
42 Macro founder and CEO Darius Dale
will weigh in with an outlook.
>> [music]
>> Stay with us.
Welcome back. Well, nervousness this
morning on Wall Street. Take a look at
futures indicating a decline at the
start of trading this morning.
The futures are not unchanged. That's
incorrect. The futures right now are
down 311. The S&P and the Nasdaq also
weaker. Oil prices meanwhile are higher.
Brent is is above Let's take a look at
where Brent is as a crude oil is also
right around 100. Brent is at 113.
That's up about 1% and crude is at 99.
Up almost 1% there as well. Markets, of
course, on edge through escalating
tensions.
And and and in particular, the Strait of
Hormuz continues to be the focus.
President Trump put a 48-hour ultimatum
on Iran to open up
the strait and allow free-flowing
tankers to transport. That ultimatum
ends tonight. 48 hours would be at
around 7:00 p.m. tonight. Investors are
bracing for supply disruptions as Iran
launches fresh strikes across the Gulf
in retaliation. Meanwhile, the
International Energy Agency says more
than 40 energy assets across the region
have been severely damaged threatening
to delay any supply recovery. The
Treasury Department suspended sanctions
on 140 million barrels of Iranian oil in
tankers at sea as Treasury Secretary
Scott Bessent told us on Friday that the
US would do that. Joining me now is 42
Macro founder and CEO Darius Dale.
Darius, good to see you in this nervous
market right now. What do you do as an
investor?
Well, good morning, Maria. Thank you for
having me. The first thing I need to
communicate to the audience is that we
are in a global liquidity crisis. And I
don't say that to scare people. I say
that to that investors can understand
what the full distribution of probable
market outcomes can be in a global
liquidity crisis. I think most of the
discussion thus far has been focused on
the current account. What will happen
with the GDP if we remove these barrels
of crude oil from the market, but I
think what we're learning from watching
the gold markets and other markets trade
in recent weeks is that this is a
capital account crisis. The Gulf Coast
economies are not getting the revenue
they need to keep you know,
supporting the bid and and and and
global equities, global credit, private
credit, etc. And so they're selling gold
to get liquidity. But this is also an
issue when you think about the net
international investment surplus
economies over in Asia that can't get
the barrels of crude oil they need to
grow their economies to generate the
profits and the household savings that
we need to continue to support in
capital global capital markets. And so
as long as this conflict persists,
investors should expect that we continue
to remain in this risk-off inflation
market regime. Well, Mohamed El-Erian
writes an op-ed this morning actually in
in the Financial Times and and he
discusses that
in basically saying, "Look, the Middle
East was was a place where we had
significant flows, significant money was
invested. And now with this disruption,
perhaps they pull back over the near
term."
But I wonder about all of these declines
that we're seeing because you're not
only seeing stocks go down, but you also
see gold and silver declining and a bit
of a freefall. Investors have exited
safe haven metal trades as well. Gold
had the worst week in 15 years last week
wiping out gains for the year. Precious
metals have been under pressure on
rising inflation fears and the potential
for higher interest rates.
The
price of gold down 6% and silver down 7
and 3/4%.
The sell-off also extended to copper,
palladium, and platinum. And I want to
mention the interest rates this morning
because yields this morning are higher.
On the 10-year, you're seeing a spike in
the yield on the 10-year by 5 and 1/2
basis points sitting at a level of 4.4%.
What's your take here? Market
expectations, I guess, are swinging. I
don't understand this that 30% of the
market is now expecting a hike in rates
as investors are are pricing in
a growing chance of a tightening here
even though I spoke with Mickey Bowman,
the vice chairman of the Federal
Reserve, on Friday and she told me that
she's still expecting three rate cuts
this year. What are you expecting?
You know, in our view, we think the next
discussion that the Fed is going to have
is going to be about hiking interest
rates. And here, just going back to your
comments on gold, this reminds me a lot
of what we saw in March of 2020 when
investors were panic selling Treasuries
to get liquidity because they couldn't
get out of the things that they should
have gotten out of. And it also reminds
me of the global financial crisis in
2008 when investment grade credit
spreads blew out wider than high yield
credit spreads because investors
couldn't sell the high yield. So they
started selling the things that they
could sell, which is investment grade.
And this And the issue The reason I
bring that up is because
back in those instances, we actually had
a central banks come and ride to the
rescue. Right now, as we learned last
week from the Fed, the Bank of England,
the European Central Bank, and the Bank
of Japan, is that central bankers are
not only not riding to the rescue,
they're actually going to be making
matters worse if you think about their
reaction functions in terms of what's
being priced into you know, money
markets across the world. In our view,
if you think about the positive
productivity shock the Fed signaled with
its summary of economic projections last
week and in the context of most recently
having an inflation price spike just a
few years ago, it's highly likely that
the Fed is talking about hiking interest
rates at some point in the next quarter
or two if this crisis persists.
So So what's your take then? Do you Do
you expect it or are you expecting that
we will still see a cut in rates?
I don't expect them to actually get to
hiking interest rates, but I do think we
are now seeing two-sided distribution.
Right now, we've had an asymmetrically
dovish reaction function out of the Fed
since going back to the fall of 2023.
Now we now have two-sided distribution,
and that's a new risk that markets have
to price in. That's all What all this
ultimately means is that the Fed put is
significantly lower than investors
realize.
All right, Darius. Thanks very much.
We'll keep watching all of that. We
appreciate it, Darius Dale.
